artifacts.content:
Hi Elena,

I’m hiding in the CR‑V in the Maple Crest parking lot for ten minutes of quiet before I go back up with the groceries, so you’re getting a brain dump.

We’re still digging out from Mateo’s big scare last year. Since February 2023, most of what we had in savings has gone straight to UW Health American Family Children’s Hospital for that $4,800 bill from his three‑night stay after he caught COVID at Eastside and the asthma attack went sideways. Between that and the $450 ER copay from when he had the flare at Chavez on 1/8/25 and we ended up at UW Health East Madison Hospital, it feels like every extra dollar goes to some hospital in this town.

On top of that, Renee’s hours at Lakeside keep getting yanked around. Remember when she tested positive again in February 2023 after that double shift and we got hit with the $780 St. Mary’s ER bill? I finally begged them into a $35/month payment plan so we wouldn’t have to choose between that and rent. Then this January her manager Mark wrote her up for calling out the no‑mask nonsense in the kitchen and pulled her off the schedule for 10 days. That was about $450 we’d counted on for Alliant, gone.

Meanwhile Lucia’s still doing OT, Mateo’s inhalers are up to like $160/month since Dr. Patel added the second controller last spring, and we’re putting Woodman’s and Walmart runs on the Discover again whenever SNAP falls short. I keep telling myself if we can just get current on rent and the utilities, we can breathe a little.

How’s MATC going? I’m still glad I could send that $300 in November so they didn’t drop you, even if it did wreck our December budget.

Love you,
Jordan

metadata.subject: Quick catch-up from the parking lot

artifacts.content:
Mami,

I just put the kids to bed and finally have a minute to write. Mateo’s breathing is okay this week, thank God. Since he had COVID back in November 2022, his asthma’s just never gone back to his old “normal.” In spring 2023, Dr. Patel at UW Health East Clinic upped his inhaler dose and added a second controller, and our part of the prescriptions jumped from about $45 a month to around $160. There were a couple months we had to put the refills on the Capital One card because we just didn’t have it.

You know a lot of this started way back when Renee got laid off for six weeks during the indoor dining shutdown in April–May 2020 at Lakeside Grill. We fell two months behind on rent at Maple Crest Apartments then, and the landlord filed that 5‑day eviction notice in Dane County Court. Even though we eventually paid the $2,100, it still shows up on our rental record and makes it hard to move somewhere cheaper.

We’re also still paying for when you were in Aurora St. Luke’s in Milwaukee with COVID in November 2020. I drove from Madison three weekends in a row and put about $900 in gas, parking, and groceries for you and Elena on the Chase Freedom card at 24% interest. That balance just hangs around, no matter how much I chip away.

But it’s not all bad. Lucia is loving her preschool at Little Sprouts and the therapists say she’s making progress with her fine motor skills. On Sundays we still do our video calls, and she shows off her drawings and Mateo reads to you in Spanish and English. Those little things help me remember why all this juggling is worth it.

Te quiero mucho,
Jordan

Title: Money/mental health spiral – jotting it all down

I need this in one place so my brain stops spinning.

Work: Since January 2021, when I had that panic attack during a virtual IEP meeting with my 7th graders and started seeing Dr. Angela Ruiz at Journey Mental Health Center on East Washington, I’ve been juggling therapy ($40 weekly co‑pay) with dropping some after‑school program hours at Eastside. That’s about $260/month less coming in. Then in October 2024, I got an informal written warning from my department chair at Eastside for missing two IEP meetings when Lucia’s preschool had a COVID outbreak. I lost a half‑day’s pay each time. It made me terrified to take any more time off, even for Mateo’s asthma follow‑ups.

December 2024 was rough: Dr. Lena Ortiz at the UW Health Behavioral Health Clinic pushed me to take three unpaid days because of the depressive episode and panic attacks from managing the 6th–8th grade caseload during hybrid. That short paycheck is why we were behind on December rent and Alliant. Around the same time, Elena called from Milwaukee short on MATC tuition and I sent her $300 from our December budget so she wouldn’t get withdrawn from the nursing program.

Cars/therapy/kids: Since September 2024, Lucia’s weekly OT at GHC‑South Clinic ($35 copay + ~$18 gas/parking every Thursday) has been good for her, but when the 2010 Civic needed a $930 brake and tire repair at Wilde East Towne Honda on 10/14, I skipped two sessions and put the rest on CareCredit. That card is now around $1,420.

Writing this out, it’s obvious: I’m burning myself out trying to be the safety net for everyone.

metadata.title: Money/mental health spiral – jotting it all down

artifacts.content:
Event: Lucia – OT @ GHC-South Clinic
Date: Thursday, 2025-02-13
Time: 3:15–4:00 p.m.
Location: GHC-South Clinic, Madison
Attendees: Jordan, Lucia

Notes:

• Weekly occupational therapy for Lucia’s mild developmental delays (started Sept 2024). $35 copay at check-in + usually around $18 for gas and parking from Maple Crest Drive to the clinic and back.
• Bring her favorite fidget and the picture schedule; ask therapist about the fine motor goals and whether we can build more practice into weekend routines.
• Keep an eye on CareCredit balance – currently about $1,420 after putting several sessions on it when the 2010 Civic needed that $930 brake and tire repair at Wilde East Towne Honda on 10/14. Already skipped two sessions in October to save money; don’t want to cancel more if we can help it.
• Coordinate with neighbor Mrs. Klein to grab Mateo off the Chavez Elementary bus at 3:25. Double-check that his inhaler is in his backpack in case he wheezes walking home in the cold.
• Dinner plan: defrost chili from freezer before we leave so there’s something ready when we get back and I’m not tempted to grab drive‑thru.
• Reminder: log mileage for this visit in the little notebook in the glove compartment so I have a rough idea what we’re actually spending on these Thursday drives.

Hi Megan,

I wanted to say thank you again for meeting with me in your office last week to set up the emergency grocery and gas assistance through the district’s family hardship fund. After UW Credit Union hit our checking account with $216 in overdraft fees when the Spectrum and T‑Mobile autopays both landed at once, we were literally staring at a negative balance and an empty fridge. I’m still embarrassed that I had to borrow $200 in cash from Ms. Patel just to get through that weekend.

It’s been a long build‑up to this point. Back when Eastside had those sudden COVID closures in January and March 2021, I burned through all my sick and personal days staying home with Mateo and Lucia and ended up taking three unpaid days in March and two in April. Then on April 19, 2021, Ms. Henderson emailed me that my attendance was “raising concerns,” even though it was all COVID childcare. That message has been in the back of my mind every time one of the kids gets sick or Mateo has an asthma flare.

More recently, in late November 2024, Dr. Lewis pulled me aside about my late arrivals when I’d been taking Mateo to his asthma appointments at UW Health East Clinic. I panicked about losing more pay and took out a $600 CashNet payday advance to cover a missed November rent payment at Ridgeview Apartments and some inhaler copays. Now that’s turned into overdraft fees, collection calls, and this feeling that I’m constantly one step away from everything collapsing.

The grocery cards you helped us get mean we can at least feed the kids while I try to untangle the rest. I really appreciate you seeing the whole picture and not just the numbers.

Best,
Jordan

metadata.subject: Thank you for your help
metadata.location: Eastside Middle School – Special Education Department

artifacts.content:
Thread with Daniel – 2024-12-06

Jordan: Hey, you free to talk later? Yesterday was a lot.

Daniel: On break. What happened?

Jordan: Mom slipped on the ice outside her apartment in Milwaukee on 12/5 and fractured her wrist. She spent two nights at Froedtert. Her Medicare Advantage still left her with a $780 bill, so I put it on my already maxed‑out Chase, plus the Greyhound trips back and forth from Madison. The card’s now 60 days past due even though they put it in a hardship program.

Daniel: Shit. Is she okay at least?

Jordan: She’s sore and mad at the ice but okay. Elena’s staying with her a few nights. I just feel like I’m drowning. This is on top of the $900 I’m still paying off from when she had COVID at Aurora St. Luke’s in November 2020 and I drove down three weekends in a row.

Jordan: And you remember October 2022 when Renee caught COVID again and missed almost three weeks at Lakeside Grill? We got a 5‑day notice at Maple Crest Apartments for being $742 behind on rent. Mom had to lend me $400 so I could sign that “last chance” payment plan with the office on 10/17 and keep it from turning into another eviction case.

Daniel: You shouldn’t have to carry all of this.

Jordan: I know. But if I don’t, who will? I’m just trying to keep Mom housed, keep our place on Maple Crest, and make sure Mateo has his inhalers and Lucia gets to preschool. Some days it feels possible, other days like yesterday… not so much.

Daniel: Call me tonight. I’ll see what I can cover.

Jordan: Thanks. I’ll put the kids down early and ring you.

Renee,

I know we talked about money last night, but I wanted to put it in writing so we’re on the same page.

Since SNAP got cut back in June 2023, we’ve been leaning on the Discover card more than we want to. By last September it was around $1,950 just from Woodman’s and Walmart on the west side, plus diapers. That’s also when I skipped my July and August 2023 student loan payments, and FedLoan Servicing sent that early delinquency notice on 9/28/23. I’m scared of those loans spiraling, but I also can’t not feed the kids.

Then there’s the car stuff. In November we had to put the CR‑V brake and tire replacement on that high‑interest store card to keep both of us able to get to Eastside and Lakeside. We were hoping to catch up on that and January rent, but Mateo’s asthma flare at Chavez on 1/8/25 and the $450 ER copay at UW Health East Madison wiped out what we’d set aside. Add in the $220 in groceries and $95 for Lucia’s Little Sprouts preschool fees we put on the store card when Lakeside shut down your evening shifts for 10 days after the COVID exposure in early January, and it’s no wonder we’re a month behind on rent.

I keep thinking about December 2024 too, when Dr. Ortiz at UW Health Behavioral Health told me to take those three unpaid days because of the panic attacks. That’s part of why we were short then, plus the $300 I sent Elena so MATC wouldn’t drop her.

None of this is about us being irresponsible. It’s just too many hits at once. I still believe if we can get rent and the highest‑interest stuff current, we can manage on my salary and your shifts.

Love you,
J

metadata.subject: Trying to see the whole picture
